primary_concept: Data persistence — how and when to use localStorage, JSON files, and a full database to keep app data between sessions
secondary_concepts: Choosing the right persistence layer for a given use case
Structuring user-generated data for storage and retrieval
Phased feature development from MVP to more capable versions
mvp_features: Log a sleep entry with a date, bedtime, and wake time
Calculate and display hours slept for each entry
Show a list of all past sleep entries
Persist entries using localStorage so data survives a page refresh
stretch_features: Migrate storage from localStorage to a JSON file so data is not browser-bound (Phase 2)
Display a weekly average hours-slept summary (Phase 2)
Migrate storage to a Supabase database to demonstrate full persistence (Phase 3)
Add a simple visual chart of sleep duration over the past 7 days (Phase 3)
forbidden_features: Any external API call (weather, health APIs, etc.) — APIs are not introduced until Module 3
LLM integration for sleep tips or analysis — LLM APIs are not introduced until Module 3
User authentication or multi-user accounts — requires database concepts beyond the module scope and adds security complexity not yet covered
Push notifications or reminders — requires platform APIs not covered in this course
Syncing data across devices without an explicit database phase — implies an API layer not yet introduced
learner_outcome: Learners will be able to add persistence to the Sleep Tracker app using localStorage, a JSON file, and a Supabase database, and explain when each storage option is the right choice for a given scenario.
instructor_talking_points: Show the problem first: add a sleep entry, refresh the page, watch it disappear — make the learner feel the pain before introducing the fix
Contrast localStorage (single browser, no setup) with a JSON file (works anywhere the app runs) to make the trade-off concrete, not abstract
When introducing Supabase, emphasize that more power means more complexity and more breaking points — set expectations before the learner tries it
Use the Sleep Tracker's natural data shape (date, bedtime, wake time, duration) to show how structured data maps to rows in a database versus key-value pairs in localStorage
scope_warnings: Adding a 'smart bedtime recommendation' feature — this requires either an LLM API (Module 3) or statistical logic that balloons scope; redirect to simply displaying the average
Pulling in a health or fitness API (e.g., Apple Health, Google Fit) — external APIs are a Module 3 concept and these integrations also require OAuth, which is out of scope for the entire course
Building a user login so multiple people can track their sleep — user authentication requires database security configuration beyond what Module 2 covers; keep the app single-user

Adding a sleep-quality rating with emoji reactions and a searchable history — each add-on looks small but together they push Phase 1 past a single coding session; hold cosmetic and search features for Phase 2

Visualizing sleep data with an interactive chart library — charting is tempting because the data fits well, but integrating a third-party JS library introduces dependency management complexity not yet covered
